title: Madelina y Los Carpinteros: Latin American Folk Music
description:
Madelina y Los Carpinteros presents an evening inspired by the socially committed Nueva Trova and Nueva Canción folk music of Cuba and Latin America. Performing standards from their repertoire, as well as unpublished compositions, the group will use well-crafted harmonies and layered acoustic instrumentation to present fresh interpretations of Latin American folk music from Chile, Argentina, the Andes Region, Puerto Rico, Dominican Republic, Cuba, and the U.S.rnrnFeaturing:rnMadeleine "Madelina" Zayas - vocals, cajón, güiro, Venezuelan maracas, bombornFernando Feña Torres - guitar, tiple, Venezuelan cuatro, charango, zampoña, percussionrnBrandon Vance - vocals, guitar, piano, accordion, saxophone, percussionrnDenis Schmidt - guitar, charango, tiple, zampoña, bongos, drumset, effectsrnrnMadelina y Los Carpinteros is comprised of Buena Trova Social Club’s Madelina Zayas and Brandon Vance and former Grupo Raiz members Fernando Feña Torres and Dennis Schmidt. The group embodies folk roots with enriched lyricism in the tradition of Nueva Trova and Nueva Canción, movements that flourished out of the liberation struggles of Latin America. Besides their original compositions, Madelina y Los Carpinteros performs compositions by Osvaldo Torres, Simón Díaz, Rafael Manríquez, Roy Brown, Juan Antonio Corretjer, Rafael Hernández, Fernando Solanas, Roberto Goyeneche, and others.rnrnLearn more: Facebookrn rnrnEVENT DETAILS

title: Ghost + Spirit: Improvisations by Ben Goldberg, Hamir Atwal & Ila Cantor
description:
Clarinetist Ben Goldberg, guitarist Ila Cantor, and drummer/percussionist Hamir Atwal present an evening of improvisations at the Red Poppy.rnrnBen Goldberg established himself as one of the most inventive and vibrant clarinetists in jazz and improvised music in 1992 with his group New Klezmer Trio. Through his many bands and compositional projects, Goldberg has been noted for his exploration into polyphony and collective improvising. He was named #1 Rising Star Clarinetist in the Downbeat Critics Poll in both 2011 and 2013. rnrnGuitarist Ila Cantor paved her way through the jazz scene as a composer, songwriter, and creative improviser. She has recorded three albums of original music, performed in several renowned venues, and received grants for merit in composition. Cantor currently resides in the Bay Area, where she collaborates with leading local artists and initiates her own creative projects.rnrnDrummer/percussionist Hamir Atwal, a Berklee College of Music graduate, began playing drums at age ten and started playing professionally at age 18. Atwal founded rock/jazz/improv group Glimpse Trio in 2010, with whom he remains active, playing over 100 shows a year. rn rnrnEVENT DETAILS

title: Resilience and the Unseen in the Context of an Art House
description: RPAH Professional Development TrackrnSpeaker Series in Community-Engaged Arts Presenting ft. Todd Thomas Brownrn rnRed Poppy Art House founder Todd T Brown headlines the Poppy's October professional development session with a conversation on resilience while making art and creating art spaces.rnrn"Zena Carlota, Red Poppy's Managing Director, asked if I would be the guest speaker for the Poppy's October professional development session. I decided I would take the opportunity to share some of the ideas/insights that lay closest to my heart, ones that have served as a guide across the years of making art and creating art spaces, but that I don't always get to talk about. Like, what is it that brings a sense of magic into the moment? Often, our most amazing (transformative) experiences of connection happen entirely unplanned, emerging out of a synchronicity of variables in a given moment such that it comes almost as a surprise. In art and in community engagement, this is something we seek–to facilitate, or invite, transformative moments of connection. But, if we can't plan spontaneity, what are we to do? How do we create space, an opening, for something yet to be known or determined? Or, what can we do to set the context, and the invitation?rnrn"Underneath these kinds of questions, for me, is a profound appreciation for the formless quality of life; life energy and life intelligence, the kind of intelligence that lives in children, in our immune systems, in plants and ecosystems of nature–a constant spring of resilience. We can't own it or control it, but we can make a little more space in our lives, and in the environments we inhabit, to let it do its work. This is the kind of inquiry I enjoy looking at, and I'm hoping we can have a conversation along these lines, and see how these things relate to the work of art houses and to a range artistic practice."rn rnAbout Our Guest:rnTodd Thomas Brown, a multi-practice artist engaged in visual arts, performance, and social pr
excerpt: RPAH Professional Development TrackrnSpeaker Series in Community-Engaged Arts Presenting ft. Todd Thomas Brownrn rnRed Poppy Art House founder Todd T Brown headlines the Poppy's October professional d...

title: Danny Green Trio: Jazz, Classical & Brazilian Convergence
description:
Pianist/composer Danny Green and his trio return to the Red Poppy to showcase their melodically charged blend of jazz, Brazilian, Latin, and classical sounds. As a rising force on the national jazz scene, Green distinguishes himself with a beautifully articulated touch and deep affinity for Brazilian music. rnrnFeaturing:rnDanny Green - pianornJustin Grinnell - bassrnJulien Cantelm - drumsrnrnSouthern California native Danny Green has garnered a reputation in the jazz community as an emerging artist whose music sounds both seasoned and fresh. After studying classical music and jazz at UC San Diego and San Diego State University, Green signed to OA2 Records and went on to win two San Diego Music Awards for best jazz album. In 2016, he released Altered Narratives, one of Public Radio International’s “Top 30 New Releases” on its “Jazz After Hours” playlist.rnrnLearn more: Website | YouTube | SoundCloudrnPhoto by: Sasha Israel Photographyrn rn“Green’s eloquent eclecticism – a gift that includes writing anything from celebratory blues to meditative reflections to composing for a string quartet – illuminates his sparkling new trio release, Altered Narratives.” – Owen McNally, WNPRrn rn

title: Miguelito Martinez: Cuba le canta a México
description:
The music of flautist Miguelito Martinez bridges the Afro-Caribbean rhythms of Cuba, the powerful melodies of Mexico, and the sounds of traditional jazz. His latest album, Cuba le canta a México, honors the musicians and composers who have contributed to these musical traditions.rnrnBorn in Chiapas, Mexico, Miguelito Martinez studied music education and transversal flute at the University of Science and Art in Chiapas and La Universidad Veracruzana, with training in Kodály and Orff. He is the founding director of the Mexican-Caribbean ensemble Corazon Sur and the co-director of Balamcoatl, a collaborative project focused on promoting the rich music and dance traditions of the indigenous people of Mexico. In 2014 he received a grant by the San Francisco Friends of Chamber of Music to produce Cuba le canta a México, an album adapting Mexican music to Caribbean rhythms and structuresrnrnLearn more: Website | Facebookrn rnrnEVENT DETAILS

title: The Brian Hingerty Group: Emerging Bay Area Jazz Talent
description:
Under the guidance of guitarist Brian Hingerty, some of the Bay Area’s finest young jazz musicians join forces to perform a contemporary take on jazz standards. The group, comprised mostly of musicians under 17 and augmented by Northern California bass giant Chris Amberger, combines elements of electronica, funk, and blues as they perform both jazz/rock classics and original compositions. rnrnFeaturing:rnBrian Hingerty - guitarrnChris Amberger - bassrnMatthew Stoloff - drumsrnJoshua Tazman - vocalsrnJasim Perales - trombonernSolomon Alber - tenor saxophonernrnWith several jazz festival honors under his belt, 17-year-old guitarist Brian Hingerty is already well established on the Bay Area music scene. Influenced by musicians like Jim Hall, Bill Frisell, John Scofield, and Derek Trucks, Hingerty draws from a wide range of genres in his exploration of American roots music. He performs regularly, both solo and in ensembles.rnrnLearn more: Website | Facebook | YouTubern rn

title: Trio Caminos: Latin Jazz Meets Spanish Beats
description:
Synthesizing elements of jazz, flamenco, tango, and Latin American music, Trio Caminos is a new acoustic ensemble led by Venezuelan guitarist Carlos Caminos. After moving to the Bay Area, Caminos connected with bassist Sascha Jacobsen and percussionist Sage Baggott to create original compositions and play music by composers such as Astor Piazzolla and Michel Camilo. Together, Trio Caminos boasts a rich, diverse repertoire that marries Latin American music to Afro-Caribbean, Spanish, and joropo beats.rnrnLearn more: Websitern rnrnEVENT DETAILS

title: 12th Annual Lit Crawl SF: Naughty, Good Magic
description:
Six literary burlesque readers tease you with each story, taking it all off, one word at a time.rnrnAbout Lit Crawl:rnThe 12th annual Lit Crawl will span over three hours in San Francisco's Mission District, featuring 99 events and over 400 performers. From fiction to nonfiction and poetry to comedy, these events will take place in bookstores, bars, galleries, restaurants, stores, cafés, and community spaces.rnrnOne of the most anticipated literary nights of the year, San Francisco’s Lit Crawl attracts close to 10,000 people, and is the world’s largest such event. In 2008, Lit Crawl NYC launched in Manhattan, also an immediate hit. Austin was next, swarming for the first time in 2011. In 2012, two more Lit Crawls were born, one in Brooklyn, another in Seattle. In 2013, the event expanded to Los Angeles, Iowa City, and London, and in 2014, Lit Crawls were launched in Miami and Helsinki.rnrnLearn more: Website | Facebook | Twitterrn rnAbout the Writers:rn rnrnNancy Au is an Oakland-based writer and co-founder of The Escapery. She graduated from UC Berkeley with a degree in Anthropology and is completing her MFA at San Francisco State University. Nancy taught creative writing at SFSU and California State University Stanislaus. Her work has appeared or is forthcoming in Liminal Stories, SmokeLong Quarterly, The Journal for Compressed Creative Arts, Necessary Fiction, Fiction Southeast, Word Riot, Identity Theory, Prick of the Spindle, and more.

rnrnCarson Beker is a writer, playwright, storyteller, and actor with an MFA and MA from SFSU. She is the co-founder of The Escapery and has taught creative writing at San Francisco State University. She is the former Fiction Editor of Fourteen Hills. Her work has appeared in Gigantic Sequins, sPARKLE + bLINK, Transfer Magazine, and Bourbon Penn, and her plays have been at the San Francisco Olympians Festival and Z Space. She will be a 2016 Lamdba Literary Resident in Fiction and is a recipient of a 2016 Tin House workshop schol

title: The pickPocket Ensemble: Lounge Music for Spies
description:
The six-piece pickPocket Ensemble returns to the Red Poppy to celebrate the release of the new album, Pepper Greenwald Presents Lounge Music for Spies. The pickPockets will play selections from the album, transporting listeners into the world of international espionage via European café music. The evening will also include remixes from the record and a set of original pickPocket tunes.rnrnFeaturing:rnRick Corrigan - accordionrnYates Brown - guitarrnChloe Allen - violinrnMatthew Souther - trumpetrnJohn Slattery - percussionrnKurt Ribak - bassrnrnInspired by musical traditions from around the globe, the pickPocket Ensemble inhabits a country all their own. The group’s charming, melodic sound allows listeners to travel the world without leaving their seats, but despite their wide range of influences, the ensemble’s music remains intensely personal and immediately engaging. In addition to their work on Lounge Music for Spies, the group has released six albums of original material.rnrnLearn more: Website | FacebookrnrnPepper Greenwald, dubbed the “second most interesting man in the world,” is the international man of mystery and consummate host who commissioned the pickPocket Ensemble to underscore the 12 stories brought back by the Global Underground Elite Spy Syndicate. His alter ego, Matt Levine, is an accomplished composer and producer of music for video games, film, and television. After graduating from the California Institute of the Arts in 1992 with a BFA in music composition, Levine worked in the music departments of Beatnik and Sony Computer Entertainment (SCE), as well as on his own as an independent composer and producer. In 2015, he joined the faculty at the San Francisco Conservatory of Music, where he teaches a course on composition for video games.rn rnrnEVENT DETAILS

title: Fémina: Argentinian Urban Folk
description:
Patagonian singer/songwriters Fémina make a stop at the Red Poppy as a part of their first-ever tour of the United States. Commingling rap and funk with Latin genres such as cumbia, candombe, rumba, and bolero, the popular trio boasts a theatrical and potent sound that unites melodic harmonies with poetic hip-hop.rnrnFeaturing:rnSofia “Toti” Trucco - vocals, guitar, ronrocornClara "Wewi" Trucco - vocals, percussionrnClara Miglioli - vocals, guitarrnrnHailing from the small mountain town of San Martín de los Andes in southern Argentina, Fémina is comprised of sisters Sofia and Clara Trucco and childhood best friend Clara Miglioli. The trio’s assertive onstage chemistry, poetic rhythms, and integration of numerous musical genres have made them popular throughout South America, Mexico, and Europe, and their first tour of the United States will take them to 15 cities. Fémina is currently working on their third full-length album, which will be released in 2017.rnrnLearn more: Facebookrn rn

title: Opaluna: Contemporary Jazz with Latin Rhythms
description:
On their debut, self-titled album, Opaluna, Colombian vocalist Susana Pineda and Bay Area guitarist Luis Salcedo present a fresh approach to contemporary jazz and Latin music, exploring every facet of their instruments while incorporating electronic techniques to create new textures and effects. The duo, joined at the Red Poppy by bassist Jeff Denson and drummer Zach Mondlick, melds their disparate backgrounds through a soulful intermingling of cultures, rhythms, and traditions.rn rnFeaturing:rnSusana Pineda - vocals rnLuis Salcedo - guitarrnJeff Denson - bassrnZach Mondlick - drums, percussionrnrnThe voice and guitar duo comprised of Susana Pineda and Luis Salcedo was formed in 2015 at the California Jazz Conservatory in Berkeley. Pineda, who hails from Medellín, Colombia, and Salcedo, a Bay Area native, explore the frontiers of folk, rock, Latin groove, as well as the many vibrant colors of jazz on Opaluna, released on Ridgeway Records.rnrnLearn more: Website | Facebook | YouTube | SoundCloudrn rn

title: CHIVES: A Bicoastal Contemporary Jazz Collective
description:
With a deep musical connection they’ve nurtured since middle school, the bicoastal trio known as CHIVES specializes in a contemporary jazz sound. Sometimes playing inside themes and sometimes soaring far beyond, saxophonist/clarinetist Steven Lugerner accompanies bassist Matthew Wohl and drummer Max Jaffe in a performance that is both elastic and buoyant. Come see this trio of native San Franciscans play a special hometown show at the Red Poppy to celebrate the release of their latest album, Yeaux.rnrnFeaturing:rnMatthew Wohl - bassrnSteven Lugerner - saxophones, clarinetsrnMax Jaffe - drumsrnrnMatthew Wohl, Steven Lugerner, and Max Jaffe grew up together in the suburbs of San Francisco, surrounded by peers and teachers that nourished their musical sensibilities. They have collaborated together in various settings for more than half of their lives. After relocating to Brooklyn, CHIVES produced and released For Brian Wilson, followed by Dads, on the Primary Records label. Their third recording, Yeaux, will be released on October 7th with a special performance at the Red Poppy Art House. CHIVES maintains an active performance schedule in both the San Francisco Bay Area and New York City.rnrnLearn more: Website | Bandcamprn rn

title: October 1st MAPP: Calling Divine Spirits / Joining Opposing Forces
description: Byb Kongo Bibene presents Nkisi Nkondi: A Divine Sculpture from Central Africa, a public talk which reveals the not so told functions of the nkisi nkondi, the sacred statues found in the great kingdom of Kongo in the 19th century. The Nkisis were power figures used by individuals, families, or whole communities to destroy or weaken evil spirits, prevent or cure illnesses, repel bad deeds, solemnize contracts or oath taking, and resolve disputes. A diviner or holy person would activate the statue, using magical substances. In this presentation, Byb shows a portrait of Central Africa before and after colonization, with social, political, and religious implications. The talk precedes a demonstration of a few dances that may have served as support in the process of calling for the divine spirits to inhabit the body of the nkisi nkondi, as practiced by the Kongo people. rnrnDynamism is Ben Baker's project for the exploration of themes relating to the deliberate bringing together of oppositional forces. He has adopted the practice of butoh to examine existentialist themes. Butoh, known as the Dance of Utter Darkness, is characterized by dancing through imagery and may be utilized to examine the ongoing battle between light and darkness.rn rnAbout the MAPP:rnLaunched in 2003, the Mission Arts & Performance Project (MAPP) is a homegrown bi-monthly, multidisciplinary, intercultural happening that takes place in the Mission District of San Francisco. On the first Saturday of every even month of the year, the MAPP transforms ordinary spaces, such as private garages, gardens, living rooms, studios, street corners, and small businesses into pop-up performance and exhibition sites for a day/night of intimate-scale artistic and cultural exchange among a kaleidoscope of individuals and communities.rn rnMAPP Schedule:rn7:00-8:00PM - Nkisi Nkondi: A Divine Sculpture from Central AfricarnDance Demonstration & Public Talk with Byb Kongo Bibenern8:10-9:10PM - DynamismrnButoh Dance-Theat

title: Rob Reich Quintet: New Music for Jazz Accordion
description: Fresh off the release of his debut album, Shadowbox, accordionist, pianist, and composer Rob Reich and his quintet return to the Red Poppy. Mixing improvisational romps with refined and melodic ensemble arrangements, Rob’s idiosyncratic accordion style mixes doses of wry humor and elements of spontaneous revelation that help propel the instrument’s modern renaissance.rnrnRob Reich (photo by Eddy Joaquim) grew up in Long Island, New York, and began studying piano at the age of three. After graduating from Oberlin Conservatory of Music with a degree in music composition, he moved to the Bay Area where he discovered the accordion. He has since become one of San Francisco’s stalwart underground artists, well-known for his work with revered chamber music ensemble Tin Hat Trio. In addition to performing live soundtrack to silent films and composing music for contortionists and trapeze artists with Circus Bella, Rob performs with the acclaimed groups Todd Sickafoose’s Tiny Resistors, Ben Goldberg School, Gaucho, Nice Guy Trio, and Prez Kids. His debut album of original compositions, Shadowbox, combines strong melodic ideas, rhythmic drive, and a spirit of irreverence and experimentation. rnrn"Rob Reich has carved out a niche as a premier avant-roots voice… like Nino Rota writing for a small jazz group.” — JAZZIZrn rnrnrnrnrnAdmission: $10-20. Doors at 7:00pm. Show at 7:30pm. All ages welcome.rnrnTickets will not be sold in advance for this event. Please arrive at the Red Poppy when doors open to purchase tickets.rnrnJoin the conversation at the Poppy – volunteer with us! Click here.

title: Wahid: Improvisations of Greece and the Levant
description: Using a modern and fluid approach to improvisation, oud player Dimitris Mahlis and frame drummer Chris Wabich explore Greek, Persian, and Indian sounds that date back to antiquity. Described by Digital Jazz News as “eclectic, organic, and unplugged in the truest sense of the word,” Wahid’s newest release, Inside Silence, synthesizes their cross-cultural music into a distinctive jazz idiom.rnrnFeaturing:rnDimitris Mahlis - oudrnChris Wabich - tuned frame drumsrn rnrnrnrnrnAdmission: $10-20. title: Pagodinhos SF: Monthly Rio-Style Pagode de Mesa
description: The Red Poppy Art House presents a monthly Rio-style pagode de mesa featuring a revolving cast of the finest players in the Bay Area. Pagode, meaning “party,” is the small group subgenre of samba that is played in samba schools, bars, cafes and backyards of Rio de Janeiro. From cavaquinho and guitar, to vocals and percussion, the musical style includes a lively mixture of songs with active audience participation.rnrnrnrnAdmission: Free, donations accepted for musicians ($5-10). Event at 1:00pm. All ages welcome.rnrnJoin the conversation at the Poppy – volunteer with us! Click here.

title: Richard Sears Quintet ft. Hard-Bop Legend Tootie Heath: CD Release
description: Taking its name from the Southern California foothills, jazz pianist Richard Sears’ Altadena is a five-part suite that features drummer Albert “Tootie” Heath in a mostly free-form musical context, blurring the line between soloist and accompanist. rnrnFeaturing:rnSteven Lugerner - alto saxophone, bass clarinet rnPatrick Wolff - tenor saxophonernRichard Sears - piano, compositionsrnGarret Lang - bassrnTootie Heath - drumsrnrnBrooklyn-based pianist Richard Sears has performed with some of the greatest jazz musicians alive, including Chick Corea, Mark Turner, Joshua Redman, and Billy Hart. His compositions have been recognized by the Aaron Copland Fund and the Los Angeles Jazz Society. In 2015, Richard released his latest trio recording, Skyline, on Fresh Sound Records.rnrnGrowing up in Philadelphia, Tootie Heath came of age playing drums with the likes of Bobby Timmons, McCoy Tyner, and Jimmy Garrison. He made his recording debut on John Coltrane’s first album, Coltrane, in 1957. Tootie, who lived in New York and Denmark through the 1960s, continued to make history working with Thelonious Monk, Nina Simone, Wes Montgomery, and Dexter Gordon, among others. Currently, Tootie lives in Santa Fe, New Mexico. He is 81 years old.rn rnrnrnrnrnAdmission: $15-25. Doors at 7:00pm. Show at 7:30pm. All ages welcome.rnrnTickets will not be sold in advance for this event. Please arrive at the Red Poppy when doors open to purchase tickets.rnrnJoin the conversation at the Poppy – volunteer with us! Click here.

title: Steph Johnson Trio: Soul, Jazz, Funk & Blues from San Diego
description: Steph Johnson brings her trio to the Red Poppy to celebrate the release of her new album, Music is Art, produced by Grammy Award-winner Kamau Kenyatta. Steph’s rich, fervent vocals and soothing guitar will be accompanied by double bassist Rob Thorsen and drummer Fernando Gomez for an evening of compassionate soul jazz.rnrnFeaturing:rnSteph Johnson - vocals, guitarrnRob Thorsen - bassrnFernando Gomez - drumsrnrnSan Diego-based Steph Johnson is a self-taught musician whose distinctive style of vocal and guitar reflects her soul, jazz, funk, and blues influences. After beginning her music career in the soul/R&B genre, Steph made a drastic leap into the jazz world in 2011 after a chance encounter with upright bassist Rob Thorsen. In 2014, she was nominated for Artist of the Year and Best Jazz Artist awards by the San Diego Music Awards. Johnson currently leads a busy performance and tour schedule with her trio, which includes Thorsen and drummer Fernando Gomez. Her latest album, Music is Art, includes nine soulful, accessible, and evocative original compositions along with “Human Race,” her take on Wayne Shorter’s classic “Night Dreamer.”rn rnrnrnrnrnAdmission: $10-15. Doors at 7:00pm. Show at 7:30pm. All ages welcome.rnrnTickets will not be sold in advance for this event. Please arrive at the Red Poppy when doors open to purchase tickets.rnrnJoin the conversation at the Poppy – volunteer with us! Click here.

title: Beyond the Blues: Ending the Prison Industrial Complex, Series 4, Discussion 6 w/ Marcus Shelby
description:
Marcus Shelby presents Discussion 6 of Beyond the Blues: Ending the Prison Industrial Complex on Wednesday, September 21, 2016. Composer Marcus Shelby and special guests continue these interactive discussions at the Red Poppy Art House on issues about mass incarceration and combating the prison industrial complex. With a focus on dealing with police violence in our communities, this meeting will feature executive director of AFRO SOLO, poet, and activist Thomas Simpson and his presentation, "Guidelines for Surviving a Police Stop." The Beyond the Blues discussions are open to anyone interested in reforming our criminal justice system and understanding how the arts can be part of that process.rnrnSan Francisco-based Marcus Shelby is an accomplished teacher, composer, arranger, and bassist. He was awarded a 2009 Black Metropolis Research Consortium Fellowship in Chicago and received the City Flight Magazine 2005 Award as one of the “Top Ten Most Influential African Americans in the Bay Area.” Active in music education, Marcus currently teaches at The Community Music Center and the Stanford Jazz Workshop. In addition to leading many of the San Francisco Jazz Festival Family Matinee Concerts, he is the artistic director/leader of The Marcus Shelby Orchestra, the music director of the Healdsburg Jazz Festival Freedom Jazz Choir, and an artist-in-residence with the Yerba Buena Gardens Festival.rn rnThe Beyond the Blues series is supported by the Yerba Buena Gardens Festival.

title: Broun Fellinis: Afro-Futurist Jazz
description: Broun Fellinis present an evening of postmodern Afro-futurist jazz featuring improvisations and compositions that span their 25-year existence. Their sound, informed by the legacy of black American music, pays homage to the creative women and men who gave us this vast library of music. From field hollers to blues, jazz, funk, hip-hop, and the experimental, Broun Fellinis are part of an ongoing aesthetic cosmic continuum that the Art Ensemble of Chicago coined “Great Black Music from the Ancient to the Future.” rnrnFeaturing:rnKirk Peterson - electric bass, effects rnKevin Carnes - drums, loopsrnDavid Boyce - tenor and soprano saxophones, effects, poetryrnrnrnrnAdmission: $10-15. Doors at 7:00pm. Show at 7:30pm. All ages welcome.rnrnTickets will not be sold in advance for this event. Please arrive at the Red Poppy when doors open to purchase tickets.rnrnJoin the conversation at the Poppy – volunteer with us! Click here.

title: Gautam Tejas Ganeshan: New Carnatic Music
description: Gautam Tejas Ganeshan’s performances reflect his love and deep knowledge of Carnatic music. His elaborate structures, extensive improvisations, and utmost sincerity distinguish him as one of the great practitioners of the form. Performed with chamber ensembles, Ganeshan’s music prioritizes authenticity over ethnicity and immediacy over nostalgia. His concerts offer listeners a rare peek into the complex song forms of a rich oral tradition.rnrnFounder and director of the Sangati Center, a non-profit chamber music concert series, Gautam Tejas Ganeshan has hosted more than 400 public chamber concerts in San Francisco, Oakland, and Berkeley since 2006. He has performed widely in the Bay Area including at SFMOMA, BAM/PFA, Asian Art Museum, as well as given workshops at the San Francisco Conservatory of Music and Stanford Jazz Workshop. He has earned support from the National Endowment for the Arts, Alliance for California Traditional Arts, and more.rn rnrnrn"Studio Grand" - 7.24.16 from Gautam Tejas Ganeshan on Vimeo.rnrnrnrnAdmission: $10-15. Doors at 7:00pm. Show at 7:30pm. All ages welcome.rnrnTickets will not be sold in advance for this event. Please arrive at the Red Poppy when doors open to purchase tickets.rnrnJoin the conversation at the Poppy – volunteer with us! Click here.

title: Ricardo Peixoto and Duo Violão Brasil: Brazilian Guitar
description: Bay Area-based Brazilian guitarist Ricardo Peixoto returns to the Red Poppy alongside Rio de Janeiro-based guitarists Rogério Souza and Edinho Gerber (Duo Violão Brasil). Performing original compositions, as well as a traditional repertoire from composers like Pixinguinha, Baden Powell, and Tom Jobim, the trio will spend the evening exploring the many styles of 20th-century Brazilian music.rnrnFeaturing:rnRicardo Peixoto, Rogerio Souza, Edinho Gerber - guitarrn rnGuitarist/composer Ricardo Peixoto is among the top representatives of Brazilian guitar in the United States today. Born in Rio de Janeiro, his fluid melodic style and keen compositional sense explore his country’s rich and diverse traditions, both in original works and arrangements of Brazilian classics. He has recorded and performed with Claudia Villela, Flora Purim and Airto, Bud Shank, Dom Um Romão, Sivuca, Toots Thielemans, Dori Caymmi, Guinga, Claudio Roditi, and Terra Sul. In addition to his work as a performer and composer, Ricardo is well respected as a teacher and lecturer on Brazillian music.rnrnA master of 6- and 7-string guitars, Rogério Souza is one of the foremost representatives of music from Rio de Janeiro. He has worked as musical director and producer with a multitude of world-class Brazilian musicians, including Baden Powell, Paulinho da Viola, Sivuca, Ney Matogrosso, Altamiro Carrilho, João Bosco, Paulo Moura, Ivan Lins, Guinga, and Turíbio Santos. Rogério performs regularly throughout Latin America, the United States, Europe, and Asia.rnrnBeing the son of a Brazilian mother and an American father, guitarist/composer Edinho Gerber possesses a rich musical vocabulary developed in the two countries. Navigating effortlessly between the genres of choro, jazz, samba, and blues, he is always in search of the intersection points within his dual cultural identity. title: Ya Layl: Explorations of the Middle East
description: Ya Layl presents “Mostly Middle Eastern,” a program of Arabic, Turkish, Armenian, and other music performed by Sarah Michael, Peter Maund, and Suellen Primost. Special guest Niko Bakulich joins the trio for an eclectic evening of lively dance numbers, modal improvisations, American standards, and medieval pilgrim songs.rnrnFeaturing:rnSarah Michael - kanun (Arabic zither)rnSuellen Primost - cellornPeter Maund - percussionrnNiko Bakulich - bassrn rnKanun player Sarah Michael has been studying and performing Middle Eastern music for the past 15 years. Beginning her career as a viola da gamba player, Sarah has studied with composer and accordion virtuoso Elias Lammam. She has performed with Aswat, Flowers of the Nile, the Mevlevi Mutrib, El Alzifoon, and her own quartet, Zamanna.rnrnPeter Maund studied percussion at the San Francisco Conservatory of Music and music, folklore, and ethnomusicology at the University of California, Berkeley. Described by the Glasgow Herald as “the most considerate and imaginative of percussionists,” he can be heard on over 50 recordings. Maund, a founding member of Ensemble Alcatraz and Alasdair Fraser’s Skyedance, has performed with a number of early and contemporary music ensembles.rnrnSuellen Primost is a conservatory-trained cellist, composer, and recording artist. She was principal cellist with the Taos Chamber Orchestra and co-founded Sedona’s first “Jazz on the Rocks” festival before moving to California in 2002. She has served as music director for Artship Ensemble in San Francisco and continues to perform with musicians, poets, and artists spanning many different genres. Suellen currently performs with Spiral Dance Chamber Jazz, the Neshamah Duo, the Sarah Michael Qanun Ensemble, and the Espiritu Ensembles.rn rnrnrnrnrnAdmission: $15-20. Doors at 7:00pm. Show at 7:30pm. All ages welcome.rnrnTickets will not be sold in advance for this event. Please arrive at the Red Poppy when doors open to purchase tickets.rnrnJoin the conversati

title: Red Poppy Art House Presents: Crossover Collaborative Residencies
description: Crossover Collaborative Residencies provides a framework to support the exploration of collaborative processes between artists in dance and music disciplines. Pairing six choreographer/dancers and six composer/musicians, the program follows a four-stage development from preliminary artists gatherings and orientation workshops, to early-stage “raw” presentations of works-in-progress, a middle-stage free community-engaged presentation, and a final performance/presentation.rnrnWith support from the Kenneth Rainin Foundation and San Francisco Arts CommissionrnrnrnrnrnrnAbout the Artists (Cohort 1):rn rnSandra Lawson-Ndu is an Oakland-based musician. As a vocalist and songwriter, she has contributed to and collaborated on a number of live and recorded projects, including works with DJ Center (Everything in Time LP) and Nomadic Wax (Diaspora Mix Tape Volume 2). She helped found and organize the Trinity International Hip Hop Festival, and in the last two years, she has been invited to participate in both the Zoo Labs Artist Residency and Revolutions Per Minute Artist Lab. Currently, Sandra is co-leading the band Bells Atlas and working on her solo project.rnrn rnrnLaura Larry Arrington is a dance-artist working in hybrids of idea and practice. Her work in dance (time/space/body/whole) pivots around a desire to orient towards the capacities in us all that can glimpse unseen and unutterable horizons. Her body is her life and her life is her work. (Photo by Robbie Sweeny)rnrn rn rnrnErika Oba is a composer, pianist/flutist, and educator based in the SF Bay Area. She is a member of the Montclair Women’s Big Band, the Mara Performance Collective, Ends Meat’ Catastrophe Jazz Ensemble, and the Oba/Bastian duo. She received her BM in Jazz Piano Performance from Oberlin Conservatory and her MA in Music Composition from Mills College.rnrn rn rnrnSAMMAY is a Bay Area-based emerging choreographer/producer and interdisciplinary performing artist of Kapampangan, Ilokano, and Bikol desce

title: Ridgeway Arts Presents: Brooklyn Jazz Vocalist Tammy Scheffer
description: Ridgeway Arts presents vocalist and composer Tammy Scheffer as a featured artist in their National/International program, which brings high-profile artists from around the globe to the Bay Area. The Brooklyn-based Scheffer joins double bassist and vocalist Jeff Denson and special guests for a night of contemporary jazz at the Red Poppy.rnrnFeaturing:rnTammy Scheffer - vocalsrnJeff Denson - double bass, vocalsrnSpecial guests TBArnrnTammy Scheffer has been a creative voice in NYC’s jazz circle for almost a decade. She is the leader of the Tammy Scheffer Sextet, whose album Wake Up, Fall Asleep was released on renowned saxophonist Greg Osby’s label Inner Circle Music. Most recently, she released a new EP with jazz-rock trio Morning Bound, hailed by the Chicago Tribune as “innovative, provocative, and alluring.”rnrnrnrnAdmission: $20. Doors at 7:00pm. title: Ampersan: Mexican Electro-Folk Rock
description: Join Ampersan in a sonic journey through the landscapes of Mexico and Latin America. Their music brings together the feelings of peasant songs, son, and psychedelica with rock, rap, and electronica.rnrnFeaturing:rnZindu Cano - jarana, acoustic guitar, vocalsrnKevin García - punteador, electric guitar, vocalsrnrnSince its inception in 2007, Ampersan has rooted their sound in the traditional acoustic music of Mexico, with forays into contemporary classical music and experimentation. The duo’s compositions have been used for dance performances, puppet theatre, documentaries, and short films. With two albums and a DVD, Ampersan has performed to great acclaim throughout Mexico, Spain, Germany, Uruguay, Argentina, Guatemala, and the U.S.rn rnrnrnrnrnAdmission: $12-15. Doors at 7:00pm. title: Taraf de Locos: Balkan-Latin Quintet
description: In celebration of a one-night reunion show at the Red Poppy, local band Taraf de Locos performs traditional folk and contemporary tunes rooted in the Balkans, Albania, Macedonia, Greece, Spain, Mexico, and Colombia. The evening will mark the group’s first performance together since the end of their last tour in 2015. rnrnFeaturing:rnTano Brock - saxophone, guitar, bata, vocalsrnMiguel Leon - cajon, bata, guira, congas, pandeiro, vocalsrnMireya Leon - bassrnCecilia Peña-Govea - trumpet, guira, vocalsrnAya Safiya - violin, vocalsrnrnTaraf de Locos derives cultural relevance and power from the established Balkan and Latin music traditions of San Francisco’s Mission District and the larger Bay Area. Formed in 2014, Taraf came together through the intersection of diverse musical backgrounds, shared Mission community, and a love of Greek, Albanian, Turkish, New Orleans, Mexican, and Cuban music. The band has since recruited bassist Mireya Leon, released a debut album, and toured the U.S.rnrnrnrnAdmission: $10-20. Doors at 7:00pm. Show at 7:30pm. All ages welcome.rnrnTickets will not be sold in advance for this event. Please arrive at the Red Poppy when doors open to purchase tickets.rnrnJoin the conversation at the Poppy – volunteer with us! Click here.

title: Gari Hegedus and Faisal Zedan: Music of Turkish, Arab & Greek Traditions
description: Master percussionist Faisal Zedan returns to the Red Poppy Art House alongside multi-instrumentalist Gari Hegedus for an evening of music rooted in Turkish, Arab, and Greek traditions. Displaying a dynamic approach to rhythm and melody, the duo enthusiastically rejuvenates classic styles of Middle Eastern drumming.rnrnFeaturing:rnGari Hegedus - Turkish and Arabic ouds, sazrnFaisal Zedan - derbakki, riqq, and frame drumsrnrnBorn in Lebanon and raised in Syria, Faisal Zedan grew up impassioned with the derbakki (Arabic drum). At the age of 15, after intensive study with a local drummer, Faisal became exposed to a wide repertoire of Arabic classical and contemporary music. Faisal is affiliated with several groups including Kan Zaman, Qadim, Stellamara, and Aswat Ensemble. In addition to his collaborations with local and international artists, Faisal has taught at music camps, conducted workshops around the world, and produced instructional DVDs on derbakki fundamentals, and Arabic percussion.rnrnGari Hegedus is an accomplished performer and composer who specializes in oud, saz, violin, and a wide range of stringed instruments from Greece and Turkey. Beginning his musical career playing the fiddle and mandolin, Gari has since performed and trained in the traditional music styles of Ireland, Scotland, France, Turkey, North Africa, and the Middle East for over 20 years. For many years, he toured with the Mevlevi Order of America, and he continues to participate in Turkish ceremonial gatherings around the country.rnrnrnrnAdmission: $15-20. Doors at 7:00pm. Show at 7:30pm. All ages welcome.rnrnTickets will not be sold in advance for this event. Please arrive at the Red Poppy when doors open to purchase tickets.rnrnJoin the conversation at the Poppy – volunteer with us! Click here.

title: Willow Steps: Indie Folk Pop
description: With simple song structures and catchy melodies, Willow Steps features folky tunes of love and heartbreak by singer/songwriter Jesse Strickman and vocalist Leiya Mahoney. The duo’s introspective harmonies are accompanied by upright bassist Schuyler Karr for the celebration of their new community-funded EP, A Truth Too Big to See. Opening the evening is an acoustic set by eclectic Nevada City songwriters Cactus Cult.rnrnFeaturing:rnJesse Strickman - vocals, acoustic guitarrnLeiya Mahoney - vocals, various instrumentsrnSchuyler Karr - bassrnrnOakland-based indie-folk duo Jesse and Leiya, best friends and fellow music lovers, launched Willow Steps in 2015 after years of collaborating onstage. Both seasoned musicians, Mahoney has founded a kirtan group and performed in choirs and bands, while Strickman has opened for Damien Jurado, Cake, and Jay Brannan.rnrnPhoto by Paul Maguirern rnrnrnrnrnAdmission: $10-15. title: Joanna Wallfisch: The Great Song Cycle
description: Internationally recognized singer-songwriter Joanna Wallfisch comes to San Francisco as part of her West Coast album release tour, which she is doing by bicycle. Beginning in Portland, OR and concluding in Los Angeles, Joanna will celebrate the halfway point at the Red Poppy. She will perform solo, accompanying herself on ukulele, piano, and loop pedal, layering vocals and melodies into textured soundscapes.rnrnLondon-born Joanna Wallfisch emerges as an important voice in the international music scene. Behind her voice is a composer who explores aesthetics that highlight her playfulness and fruitful imagination. A multi-instrumentalist and master of vocal looping, she creates striking, multi-faceted live performances, generating beguiling vocal and percussive soundscapes to accompany her self-penned songs. Her third and latest album, Gardens in My Mind (2016), is set to be released on Sunnyside Records.rnrn“[With her] clear-eyed poetry... Wallfisch combines the intimate purview of a singer-songwriter with the technical liberties of a jazz improviser. Crossing borders and flouting rules seem to come naturally to the youngest child of a renowned classical-music dynasty” — The Boston Globern rnrnrnrnrnAdmission: $10-15. Doors at 7:30pm. Show at 8:00pm. All ages welcome.rnrnTickets will not be sold in advance for this event. Please arrive at the Red Poppy when doors open to purchase tickets.rnrnJoin the conversation at the Poppy – volunteer with us! Click here.

title: Mamadou and Vanessa: Mali Blues
description: Mamadou and Vanessa perform original, poignant music from Mali. Their sound, influenced by West African rhythms and southern American blues, features the traditional 14-stringed kamele ngoni (hunter's harp) and the karinye, a traditional percussion instrument. rnrnMaster kamele ngoni player Mamadou Sidibe is from the Wassoulou region of Mali, West Africa. He has toured with Malian greats Oumou Sangaré and Coumba Sidibé throughout Europe and Africa. Vanessa Sidibe, from New York, is an accomplished Afro-Cuban and salsa musician. They are joint winners of several prestigious music competitions, including a Billboard world music competition and the John Lennon Songwriting Contest.rnrnrnrnAdmission: $15-20. Doors at 7:00pm. title: Marta Sanchez Quintet: Elegant, Melodic Music for Jazz Quintet
description: Combining elements of rock, pop, jazz, folk, and classical, pianist/composer Marta Sanchez creates music that is rhythmically complex and spontaneously melodic. Her most recent release, Partenika, has been hailed as one of the best albums of 2015 by The New York Times, Downbeat, AllMusic and Jazz Journalist Association. Sanchez, a Madrid native who is currently active in New York City’s contemporary music scene, continues to tour around the globe as both a bandleader and sideman.rnrnFeaturing:rnMarta Sanchez – piano, compositionsrnCaleb Curtis – alto saxophonernRaffi Garabedian – tenor saxophonernDave Ewell – bassrnEvan Hughes – drumsrnrnPhoto by Tayla NebeskyrnrnrnrnrnrnAdmission: $10-20. title: Jessica & Tony Jones, Peter Apfelbaum & Howard Wiley: Tenor Madness
description: Improvisations, originals, and an exploration of standards unite the four independent viewpoints of tenor saxophonists Jessica Jones, Tony Jones, Peter Apfelbaum, and Howard Wiley. With a deep respect for jazz history and rhythm, and a dedication to finding their own voices, these musicians meet with collective improvisation in its wildest, mercurial incarnation.rnrnFeaturing:rnJessica Jones, Tony Jones, Peter Apfelbaum, Howard Wiley - tenor saxophonesrn rnSpearheaded by Jessica Jones, this grouping of four strong, creative tenor sax players from Berkeley illustrates the power of unity across diverse voices. A shared love of the saxophone, self-expression and jazz brings this crew together to explore sonic territory inhabited by shared history and imagined landscapes. All products of Berkeley High School, Jessica, Tony and Peter currently live in Brooklyn performing music from around the world. Howard has been a recognized saxophonist/drummer in the Bay Area and beyond since he was 12 years old.rn rnPhotos: Peter Apfelbaum by Tom Erlich, Howard Wiley by James Barry KnoxrnrnrnrnAdmission: $10-20. Doors at 7:00pm. Show at 7:30pm. All ages welcome.rnrnTickets will not be sold in advance for this event. Please arrive at the Red Poppy when doors open to purchase tickets.rnrnJoin the conversation at the Poppy – volunteer with us! Click here.

title: Rumberos de Radio Habana: Community Rumba
description: Rumba music originated in the Afro-Cuban neighborhoods of Havana and Matanzas as the soundtrack to community, partnered dancing, and get-togethers as a means of cultural expression. Its global diaspora means rumba rhythms have found their way into other musical styles, such as country, rock, and blues, and continue to impact the genres of today. Rumberos de Radio Habana bring their own contribution to the Red Poppy for a free afternoon of beat-making, hip-shaking, and good company.rnrnrnrnAdmission: Free, donations accepted for musicians ($5-10). Event at 2:00pm. All ages welcome.rnrnJoin the conversation at the Poppy – volunteer with us! Click here.

title: Jeff Denson Quartet: Jazz CD Release Celebration
description: Bay Area-based double bassist/composer/vocalist Jeff Denson performs a CD release show with his quartet at the Red Poppy Art House for his fifth album, Concentric Circles. Steeped in the jazz tradition, Denson’s music features uncommon instrumentation and a highly individual juxtaposition of composed and improvised material.rnrnFeaturing:rnJeff Denson - double bass, vocalsrnPaul Hanson - bassoonrnDan Zemelman - pianornAlan Hall - drum setrnrnJeff Denson has balanced a full career as an acclaimed performing musician, a professor at the California Jazz Conservatory, and the Artistic Director of Ridgeway Arts, Inc., a 501(c)(3) non-profit arts organization. Jeff has toured with his own groups throughout the U.S. and Europe and has worked with some of jazz’s finest artists, such as Anthony Davis, Joe Lovano, Jane Ira Bloom, Kenny Werner, Charles McPherson, Dave Douglas, Ralph Alessi, Cuong Vu, and Warren Wolf.rn rn“From his hard-driving quarter-note swing, to cello-like bowed melodies and interactive counterpoint, Denson is always surprising and inspiring!” — Lee Konitz, National Endowment for the Arts Jazz Masterrn rnrnrnrnrnAdmission: $20. Doors at 7:30pm. Show at 8:00pm. All ages welcome.rnrnrnrnJoin the conversation at the Poppy – volunteer with us! Click here.

title: Lavay Smith: The Songs and Sounds of Duke Ellington
description: Internationally recognized chanteuse Lavay Smith and her world-class band come to the Red Poppy to pay tribute to the great Duke Ellington. Performing popular favorites, as well as obscure gems, Lavay and her band evoke an authentic feeling of classic blues and jazz.rn rnFeaturing:rnLavay Smith - vocalsrnJules Broussard - alto and tenor saxrnDanny Armstrong - trombone, vocalsrnChris Siebert - piano rnRon Belcher - bassrnrnLavay Smith is the vocalist and bandleader of the San Francisco-based Red Hot Skillet Lickers, one of the top swingin’ jazz and blues bands in the world. Growing up in Southern California and the Philippines, Lavay developed a singing style influenced by greats like Billie Holiday, Dinah Washington, Bessie Smith, and Little Esther Phillips. Her lush, bluesy vocals and enthralling stage personality recalls an era of jazz queens and sensuous pinups in the authentic style of the 1940s and 1950s. Smith and her seven-piece band maintain a busy schedule, performing original compositions, as well as a large repertoire of classic tunes throughout the United States, Canada, Europe, and Japan.rnrn“A living testament to the jump-blues era, Lavay is an impassioned singer.” — JAZZIZrnrnrnrnAdmission: $15-20. Doors at 7:00pm. Event at 7:30pm. All ages welcome.rnrnrnrnJoin the conversation at the Poppy – volunteer with us! Click here.

title: CANCELLED - Soup SF: Summer Edition
description: Due to unforeseen circumstances, this event has been cancelled. We apologize for any inconvenience!rnrnrnrnSOUP is like Shark Tank meets Kickstarter for social good in our community. Come together with great folks over a delicious meal and fund a creative, community-benefit project with your vote!rnrnHow It Works:rn1) $15-25 donation gets you soup and a vote (no one will be turned away for lack of funds)rn2) Three creative community-benefit projects present and answer our Q&Arn3) We vote to grant the proceeds from the meal to the most inspiring projectrnrnrnrnMore About Soup SF:rnSoup SF is a volunteer effort and exercise in community, food, creativity and good times. The SF edition was instigated by a group of folks that were wanting for more chances to be with and participate in their community. Inspired by stories of FEAST in Brooklyn and InCUBATE’s original Sunday Soup in Chicago, they decided to bring the goodness to SF (with blessings).rnrnRead about Soup SF’s previous projects here.rnJoin the Facebook event here.

title: August 6th MAPP: "Surface" Artist Reception & Crossover Collaborative Residencies
description:
The exhibit Surface: Rodney Ewing & Joyce Nojima brings together works on paper by the two Bay Area artists. Ewing and Nojima have created work offering a transmutation of tactile and visual experience across the surface of the paper. This pairing allows for exploration of the various ways in which this familiar material is treated and how the marks made upon the surface may evoke diverse readings and interpretations. The curators, Modesto Covarrubias and Monica Lundy, present these works in the performance space of Red Poppy Art House with an aim to spark a dialogue between music, voice, movement, and the act of seeing. With an interest in how to interact and live with art, the curators seek out work that enlivens and engages the space in which it exists.rnrnCrossover Collaborative Residencies provides a framework to support the exploration of collaborative processes between artists in dance and music disciplines. Pairing six choreographer/dancers and six composer/musicians, the program follows a four-stage development from preliminary artists gatherings and orientation workshops, to early-stage “raw” presentations of works-in-progress, a middle-stage free community-engaged presentation, and a final performance/presentation. The third month of the residency utilizes the Mission Arts & Performance Project’s bimonthly neighborhood festival as a platform to put the work before a larger and more spontaneous audience.rnrnSchedule:rnrn1:00-4:00pm - Surface ExhibitionrnArtist reception with curators Modesto Covarrubias and Monica Lundy and artists Rodney Ewing and Joyce Nojimarn6:00-7:30pm - Crossover ResidenciesrnCollaborative works in progress with resident artists Daniel Riera/Latanya Tigner; Erika Oba/Sammay Dizon; Sandra Ndu/Larry Arringtonrn7:30-8:00pm - Crossover ResidenciesrnDiscussion with resident artists and curator Amara Tabor-SmithrnrnrnrnAbout the MAPP:rn

title: Jacqui Sutton with the Frontier Jazz String Quartet: Jazz and Bluegrass Swing Together
description: Jacqui Sutton returns to the Red Poppy with her Frontier Jazz String Quartet. Imagine if Billie Holiday, Dolly Parton and Aaron Copland came together to create music for a night; this would be the sound of Jacqui Sutton. Described by music/arts critic C. Michael Bailey as the place “where jazz meets the American frontier,” Jacqui draws from over 20 years of musical and stage experience, interweaving jazz and bluegrass with orchestral, pop and Americana influences. In her ears, the banjo, mandolin and fiddle continually intermingled with jazz, eventually coalesce into her distinctive frontier jazz sound. She was recently featured in Chris Becker’s Freedom of Expression: Interviews with Women in Jazz (November 2015), a book discussing the contributions made by women in the jazz world.rnrnFeaturing:rnJacqui Sutton - vocalsrnPaul Chester - banjo, guitarrnAnthony Sapp - bassrnMax Dyer - cellornHenry Darragh - pianornrn*This performance is proudly sponsored by Ifshin Violins.rn rnrnrnrnrnAdmission: $20. Doors at 7:00pm. Show at 7:30pm. All ages welcome.rnrnTickets will not be sold in advance for this event. Please arrive at the Red Poppy when doors open to purchase tickets.rnrnJoin the conversation at the Poppy – volunteer with us! Click here.

title: Los Vega: Son Jarocho de Veracruz
description: Hailing from Veracruz, México, Los Vega brings the tradition of son jarocho music and dance to the Red Poppy. The genre is rich in narrative and performed with traditional instruments such as the jarana, requinto, and the percussive rhythm of zapateado dancing. Los Vega hopes to preserve the rich history of this music and promote the culture of southern Veracruz with notable songs such as Las Poblanas, El Celoso, Zapateado, Balaju, Cascabel and the original Bamba.rn rnFeaturing:rnFredi Vega - jaranarnClaudio Vega - requinto rnRaquel Palacios - jarana rnRosario Cornejo - zapateadornrnFor more than six generations the Vega family has recreated son jarocho in community fandangos and on stages across the world. Since evolving into "Los Vega", the new generation has carried on the musical tradition with contemporary verses, a variation of tones, and further stories that invigorate the family’s profound roots.rn rnrnrnrnrnAdmission: $20-25 door / $20 online. Doors at 7:30pm. Show at 8:00pm. All ages welcome.rnrnrnrnJoin the conversation at the Poppy – volunteer with us! Click here.

title: Jaz Sawyer: Abstract Jazz and Instrumental Experiments
description: An integration of abstract jazz with breakbeats and improvised grooves, Jaz Sawyer explores an instrumental experiment including both originals and arrangements of jazz and pop standards.rnrnFeaturing:rnJaz Sawyer - drums, percussionrnSarah Voynow - harprnCava Menzies - piano, keyboardsrnImalu Menzies - percussion, keyboardsrnrnJaz Sawyer began playing drums at the age of two in the Haight Ashbury District of San Francisco. His musical studies have taken him through the various programs at the Ruth Asawa School of the Arts, San Francisco Conservatory of Music, SFYO, Stanford Jazz Workshop, and The New School for Jazz and Contemporary Music. Jaz has worked with many notable artists including Wynton Marsalis, Abbey Lincoln, George Benson, Bobby Hutcherson, Phil Lesh, and Dee Dee Bridgewater in some of the world’s premier concert venues.rnrnSarah Voynow has performed extensively with the San Francisco Ballet Orchestra, the San Francisco Symphony, the San Francisco Opera Orchestra, and the Metropolitan Opera Orchestra. She earned a Doctor of Musical Arts Degree from Juilliard and has performed in numerous recordings at Skywalker Sound. As a performer with the ensemble Richard Stoltzman and Woody Herman’s Thundering Herd, she played on the Grammy-nominated recording of Stravinsky’s Ebony Concerto. A highly versatile musician, Sarah also plays rock and roll with an electric harp.rnrnBay Area native Cava Menzies is a classically trained jazz pianist, composer, producer, and educator. In 2001, she received a BA from the Berklee College of Music in Boston for jazz piano and music education, and in 2008, she received her MM from the University of Miami where she studied composition and music production and recording. Her musical talents extend from collaborations with various hip hop, soul, jazz, and Latin artists, to film documentaries. Cava remains a founding faculty member and teacher at the Oakland School for the Arts (OSA) where she chairs the Music Department, overseein

title: Michael Zilber: A Saxophonist's Tribute to Jazz Greats
description: A tribute to saxophone giants, San Francisco’s Michael Zilber performs music from his upcoming album of all originals. The recording features New York jazz greats Dave Kikoski on piano, James Genus on bass, and Clarence Penn on drums. Accompanying Zilber at the Red Poppy is a group made up exclusively of New York expatriates, including pianist Matt Clark, bassist Peter Barshay, and drummer Akira Tana.rnrnSaxophonist and composer Michael Zilber is described by NEA Jazz Master David Liebman as "one of the best players and composers around anywhere. Period!" The Canadian-born ex-New Yorker has 7 CDs to his name as a leader and has also been a sideman on a myriad of recordings. He co-led a band with drumming great Steve Smith for 8 years and throughout his career has performed, toured, and recorded with numerous leading jazz artists such as Liebman, Miroslav Vitous, and Mike Clark among many others.rn rnrnrnrnrnAdmission: $10-20. Doors at 7:00pm. Show at 7:30pm. All ages welcome.rnrnTickets will not be sold in advance for this event. Please arrive at the Red Poppy when doors open to purchase tickets.rnrnJoin the conversation at the Poppy – volunteer with us! Click here.

title: Scott Amendola Trio: Original Music & Sonic Soundscapes
description: The Scott Amendola Trio presents original music from Scott as well as a selection of cover songs. From structured forms to more outside-the-box sonic soundscapes, the trio's breadth of possibilities are endless.rnrnFeaturing:rnKarl Evangelista - guitarrnJason Hoopes - electric bass rnScott Amendola - drums/electronicsrnrnFor Scott Amendola, the drum kit isn’t so much an instrument as a musical portal. As an ambitious composer and savvy bandleader for some of the world’s most inventive musicians, Amendola applies his wide-ranging rhythmic virtuosity to a vast array of settings. His closest musical associates include guitarists Jeff Parker, Nels Cline, Charlie Hunter and Henry Kaiser, Hammond B-3 organist Wil Blades, ROVA saxophonist Larry Ochs, and Tin Hat clarinetist Ben Goldberg ‒ players who have each forged a singular path within and beyond the realm of jazz. While rooted in the San Francisco Bay Area scene, Amendola has woven a dense and far-reaching web of bandstand relationships that tie him to influential artists in jazz, blues, rock, and new music.rn rn“...drummer/signal-treater Scott Amendola is both a tyrant of heavy rhythm and an electric-haired antenna for outworldly messages (not a standard combination).” - Greg Burk, LA WeeklyrnrnFollow Scott on Facebook.rnrnrnrnAdmission: $15-20. Doors at 7:00pm. Show at 7:30pm. All ages welcome.rnrnTickets will not be sold in advance for this event. Please arrive at the Red Poppy when doors open to purchase tickets.rnrnJoin the conversation at the Poppy – volunteer with us! Click here.

A remarkable performer and devoted educator, she has spent the last three years immersed in the Šaban Bajramović songbook. title: Tito Gonzalez y Su Son de Cuba: Una Noche Cubana
description: Tito y Su Son de Cuba takes you back to old Havana, interpreting and redefining classic Cuban son at its best. With his mastery of the tres guitar, Tito Gonzalez combines the traditional music of Cuba including chachacha, son montuno, guaracha, bolero and danzon.rnrnFeaturing:rnTito Gonzalez - guitar, tres, lead vocalsrnDavid Frazier - congornSandy Poindexter - violinrnRubin Hurtado - pianornAntonio Garcia - bassrnrnSinger/composer Tito Gonzalez is a master showman with a voice that has been compared to that of classic son singer Daniel Santos. Gonzalez delivers songs that are deceptively simple – between their fluid melodies and retrograde rhythms, they evoke a focus and introspection that Tito attributes, in part, to his life in America. Along with son, his music demonstrates a broad range of Cuban genres such as rumba, guaracha and the most romantic boleros. His latest album, La Vida Te Da Sorpresas, features a contemporary take on classic Cuban son backed by extraordinary talent in his band.rnrnrnrnAdmission: $15-20. Doors at 7:00pm. Show at 7:30pm. All ages welcome.rnrnTickets will not be sold in advance for this event. Please arrive at the Red Poppy when doors open to purchase tickets.rnrnJoin the conversation at the Poppy – volunteer with us! Click here.

title: Ultrafaux w/ Rob Reich: Jazz Manouche & Musette Waltzes
description: Drawing influences from manouche, Romani and Balkan music, Ultrafaux joins together with accordionist Rob Reich and chanteuse Alexis Tantau for an evening of traditional musette, Piaf tunes, and originals.rnrnFeaturing:rnMichael Joseph Harris - guitarrnSami Arefin - guitarrnEddie Hrybyk - upright bassrnRob Reich - accordion (featured artist)rnAlexis Tantau - vocals (featured artist) rn rnBaltimore band Ultrafaux performs original music with musette, swing manouche, funk, blues, and Dixieland influences. The band is an acoustic powerhouse of Sami Arefin and Michael Joseph Harris trading dazzling leads on guitar and Eddie Hrybyk on upright bass who brings his passion for improvisation to the group. Ultrafaux’s song Latcho Dromo from their 2014 release was chosen to be featured on Django Festival #9, an international compilation of Django Reinhardt-inspired music by legendary producer Jon Larsen in Norway.rnrnAccordionist Rob Reich is quintessentially what makes the San Francisco music scene a vibrant and vital mecca for independent music; he’s become one of its stalwart underground artists. His music combines strong melodic ideas, rhythmic drive, and a spirit of irreverence and experimentation. Reich is best known for his work with Tin Hat, Gaucho, and Circus Bella.rn rnrnrnrnrnAdmission: $10-20. Doors at 7:00pm. Show at 7:30pm. title: The Village Road Trio: Folk-Inspired Classical Music
description: The Village Road Trio reimagines classical music with their folkloric instrumentation for a program of spirited and expressive sounds. From American bluegrass and swing, to Romanian tunes and favorite classical melodies, the trio takes on an extensive range of styles.rnrnFeaturing:rnAlisa Rose - violinrnRob Reich - accordionrnDaniel Fabricant - bassrn rnBassist Daniel Fabricant grew up in Ashland, Oregon, where he began playing guitar and trumpet in grade school. Since moving to the Bay Area ten years ago and transitioning to double bass, he has performed as a freelance sideman for numerous national acts. Locally, he is a member of the SF Weekly “Best Jazz Band” award-winning Nice Guy Trio, Los Boleros, Lavay Smith and the Red Hot Skillet Lickers, and other classical chamber groups, orchestras, and dance bands. He has worked various theater companies as well as in high school and university musical theater productions.rnrnRob Reich grew up in Long Island, New York studying piano from age three. After graduating with a degree in Music Composition from Oberlin Conservatory of Music, he moved to the San Francisco Bay Area, where he discovered the accordion, now his primary instrument. Whether it’s composing art songs based on the poetry of E. E. Cummings with Tin Hat, writing circus music for Circus Bella, or pioneering new jazz repertoire for accordion with the Rob Reich Trio, his music combines strong melodic ideas, rhythmic drive, and a spirit of irreverence and experimentation.rnrnFiddler and violinist, composer and improviser, Alisa Rose easily navigates between many musical styles. With roots in classical music as well as American traditional music, she attracts attention with her expressive lyrical voice on the violin. Alisa is a member of the Real Vocal String Quartet, Supermule, and the Club Foot Orchestra. From 2008 to 2012, she was a member of Quartet San Francisco; their record QSF Plays Brubeck was nominated for a Grammy in 2009, for which Alisa was featur

title: Shades of Love: Padams & Javalis
description: In this concert, Ganesh Vasudeva and Snigdha Venkatramani showcase a selection of poems from South India through Bharatanatyam, a form of Indian classical dance that originated in the temples of Tamil Nadu. These male-oriented and heroine-based poems are taken up to portray myriad shades of love.rnrnFeaturing:rnGanesh Vasudeva - abhinaya (miming)rnSnigdha Venkatramani - abhinaya, vocalsrnSrinivas Pisharath - vocalsrnAkshay Venkatesan - percussion rnNyantara Narasimhan - violinrnrnGanesh Vasudeva, one of the Bay Area's leading Bharatanatyam dancers, has trained for 30 years with renowned teachers and has performed extensively in India, the U.S. and France. Over past few years, Ganesh has choreographed and presented his own work to critical acclaim. As a researcher, his interests include male-oriented compositions that are suitable to present on stage.rn rnrnrnrnrnAdmission: $15. Doors at 7:00pm. Show at 7:30pm. title: Beyond the Blues: Ending the Prison Industrial Complex, Series 4, Discussion 5 w/ Marcus Shelby & Jackie Ramos
description:
The Red Poppy Art House is pleased to present the fourth series of Beyond the Blues: Ending the Prison Industrial Complex on March 16, April 20, May 18, June 8, and July 7. Marcus Shelby and Jackie Ramos return to delve into our justice system, its flaws, and show how music and the arts can be used as agencies for reform and change—both in front and behind bars. These interactive discussions explore the blues, the prison industrial complex, mass incarceration, the school-to-prison pipeline, restorative justice, public health, families impacted by incarceration, immigration, and more.rnrnThe discussions will feature guest speakers/artists, readings, recorded and live music, poetry, and videos. These events are open to anyone interested in reforming our criminal justice system and understanding how the arts can be part of that process.rnrnSeries 4 Discussion Schedule:rnMar 16 – Racial Disparities in the Criminal Justice System: A Call for ReformrnApr 20 – Incarceration and Its Connection to ImmigrationrnMay 18 – Ending the Death PenaltyrnJune 8 – Community Building and Restorative JusticernJuly 7 – Music and the Arts as Weapons of Critical Resistancern rnThe Beyond the Blues series is supported by the Yerba Buena Gardens Festival.

rn rnMarcus Shelby is an accomplished teacher, composer, arranger, and bassist who currently lives in San Francisco, California. Shelby was awarded a 2009 Black Metropolis Research Consortium Fellowship in Chicago to conduct research for his commission to compose “Soul of the Movement”—a 12 part musical suite on MLK and the Civil Rights Movement—and received the City Flight Magazine 2005 award as one of the “Top Ten Most Influential African Americans in the Bay Area”. Marcus is also very active in music education and currently teaches at The Community Music Center and the Stanford Jazz Workshop. In addition to leading many of the San Francisco Jazz Festival Family Matinee Concerts, Marcus is the artistic director/leader of The Marcus Shelby

title: Sound Underground: Acoustic Chamber Jazz from Miami
description:
Miami-based trio Sound Underground traverses the lengths of acoustic jazz and beyond. Intimate and conversational, their music ranges from from artful compositions and introspective ballads, to unusual instrumentation and clever sonic explorations.rnrnFeaturing:rnJonah Udall - guitarrnDavid Leon - alto saxophonernAlec Aldred - trumpetrnrnSound Underground was formed as a natural extension of the shared musical explorations and mutual admiration of three roommates with disparate backgrounds. In 2014, they released their self-titled debut with an Eastern European tour, performing at major international jazz festivals in Bulgaria and Serbia. The trio has been on the rise ever since, being recognized in Downbeat Magazine and touring on both sides of the U.S. – including their first appearance in California last year. Their sophomore effort, Quiet Spaces, will be released this summer.rnrnFollow Sound Underground on Facebook and Bandcamp.rnCheck out a performance of See You Never at the Bansko Jazz Festival below:rn

title: Sparks Fly: 21st Century Street Jazz ft. Saxophonist Avram Fefer w/ Vijay Anderson & Dan Seamans
description: Avram Fefer’s explosive trio work has been well documented over the years with critically acclaimed albums and international tours. He draws from the musical worlds of Ornette Coleman, Miles Davis, Fela Kuti, Ethiopiques, and Charles Mingus. With tunes that create fertile ground for development and improvisation, Avram’s music encompasses soulful intelligence, lyricism, and groove.rnrnFeaturing:rnAvram Fefer - saxophones, bass clarinet, compositionrnVijay Anderson - drumsrnDan Seamans - bassrnrnSaxophonist Avram Fefer spent his formative years in the company of musicians like Roy Hargrove, John Medeski, and Jeff Parker. He made his professional debut with Archie Shepp, the Last Poets, Sonny Murray and Tony Allen, and has since played with David Murray, William Parker, Marc Ribot, and Vernon Reid among many others. Avram has performed in over twenty countries, released eleven albums as a leader, and has recently been presenting the Resonant Sculpture Project which features his solo musical interactions with the sculptures of Richard Serra. His latest album, Big Picture Holiday: Shimmer and Melt, is a six-piece electric groove project.rnrnFollow Avram on Facebook and YouTube.rnrnrnrnAdmission: $10-20. Doors at 7:00pm. Show at 7:30pm. All ages welcome.rnrnTickets will not be sold in advance for this event. Please arrive at the Red Poppy when doors open to purchase tickets.rnrnJoin the conversation at the Poppy – volunteer with us! Click here.

title: 3 Leg Torso: Cinematic Chamber Music with Punk Attitude
description: Formed in 1996, 3 Leg Torso began as a violin, cello and accordion trio with the mission of creating original, contemporary chamber music for their distinctive instrumentation. Over the following years, the ensemble has expanded its musical mission and size to become a quartet that performs an eclectic synthesis of chamber music, tango, klezmer, Latin, and Roma music. The cinematic nature of their compositions has led to work with many filmmakers, including Oscar-nominated documentary artist Morgan Spurlock (Super Size Me), Teller (of Penn & Teller), Academy Award-winning animator Joan Gratz (The Dowager's Feast and The Dowager's Idyll), and more.rnrnFeaturing:rnBéla R. Balogh - violinrnCourtney Von Drehle - accordionrnT.J. Arko - percussionrnMike Murphy - double bassrnrnPreview 3 Leg Torso's music here.rnrnrnrnAdmission: $15-20. Doors at 7:00pm. Show at 7:30pm. title: Jure Pukl Trio: East Coast Tenor Sax
description: Performing a repertoire of standards and originals, Jure Pukl Trio invites you for a night of swinging music with a hint of contemporary jazz.rnrnRecipient of the Slovenian highest national award for arts (Nagrada Presernovega sklada 2015), Jure Pukl is one of the most prolific and creative saxophonists of the younger Slovene jazz generation. He obtained university education abroad studying both classical and jazz saxophone. Throughout his studies, Pukl performed and recorded extensively with his own projects as well as with a number of great musicians and orchestras, including Maceo Parker, Branford Marsalis, Dave Liebman, Vijay Iyer, Jeff "Tain" Watts, Big Band RTV Slovenia, and European Movement Jazz Orchestra among many others. In 2005, he won the first Jury Prize and Best Composition Audience Award at the Jazzon International Music Workshop and Festival in Novo Mesto, Slovenia. His auctorial projects, such as the Virus Quintet, Pukl creates a special combination of avant garde, free jazz and impressionistic contemporary music that gives prominence to the interplay between his band members.rnrnFeaturing:rnJure Pukl - tenor saxophonernHans Glawischnig - bassrnAndre Sumelius - drumsrnrnrnrnAdmission: $10-20. Doors at 7:30pm. Show at 8:00pm. All ages welcome.rnrnTickets will not be sold in advance for this event. Please arrive at the Red Poppy when doors open to purchase tickets.rnrnJoin the conversation at the Poppy – volunteer with us! Click here.

title: Mostly Kosher: Indie-Global Collective
description:
Mostly Kosher, a Los Angeles-based indie-global collective band, radically reconstructs Judaic and American cultural musical roots. Through ravenous post-klezmer beats and arresting Yiddish refrains, Mostly Kosher highlights the architecture of heritage music with influences of jazz, Latin, rock, hip hop and folk.rnrnMostly Kosher has accumulated an international legion of fans and is on a trajectory to bring their rhythmic hullabaloo to all of the streaming sound waves across the planet. The band most recently graced the stage of the Dorothy Chandler Pavilion for a live television broadcast to over half a million viewers. Their self-titled debut album has won international acclaim through recognition by World Music Network, Songlines Magazine and BBC radio.rnrnFeaturing:rnLeeav Sofer (bandleader) - vocals, piano, clarinet, percussionrnJanice Mautner Markham - violinrnMichael Bolger – trumpet, accordionrnEric Hagstrom - drums, aux percussionrnMike King - trombonernAdam Levy - upright bassrnShane Savala - guitarrnrnFollow Mostly Kosher on Facebook and YouTube.rnWatch their music video for Ikh Hob Dikh Tsufil Lib below!rn

title: [DOUBLE BILL] Rebecca Mimiaga and Bird Call: A Night of Cinematic Chamber Pop
description: Born and raised in California, Rebecca Mimiaga has been performing her own music since age 16, fronting various rock, folk and blues projects. She completed her BFA in Vocal Performance Studies at the New School For Jazz and Contemporary Music in New York City. Her music and debut EP, Words in the Dark, feature strong, intimate vocals set to piano and orchestral arrangements. When she's not Rebecca Mimiaga, she is the voice and writer for LOUIZA, her loud indie-rock project. (Photo: Matt Mimiaga)rnrnChiara Angelicola is an American singer-songwriter and musician known for her work in the musical projects Motel Pools and most notably Bird Call. Bird Call explores a range of emotional territory with Angelicola’s voice, vulnerable lyrics and candid stage presence whilst providing an aural backdrop that is variably danceable, electronically driven and, at times, futuristic and experimental. rnrnFollow Rebecca Mimiaga on Facebook and SoundCloud.rnFollow Bird Call on Facebook and SoundCloud.rnrnrnrnAdmission: $20. description: Electronic producers/singers Ben Zaidi and RZN8R join together for a night of future-soul and sonic grooves.rnrnFeaturing:rnBen Zaidi - vocals, keyboards, electronicsrnTree Palmedo - trumpetrnRZN8R - vocals, controllersrnAustan Found - electric guitarrn rnSeattle-based electronic artist Ben Zaidi (Photo: Riley Corr) creates intimate, plaintive songs in his bedroom studio. He spent several years studying poetry and creative writing at Harvard University before returning to his hometown of Seattle this past year to begin recording and performing full-time. For this performance, his self-produced songs will be brought to the world with the help of Portland jazz prodigy Tree Palmedo.rnrnRZN8R (pronounced “resonator”) is the moniker of Oakland-raised producer/singer Derek Buckwalter. title: Americano Social Club: Music for La Dolce Vita!
description: The Americano Social Club is led by Michael Zisman on mandolin and features guitarist Jason Vanderford and bassist Joe Kyle Jr. In addition to performing their own dramatic original pieces, they play music for "la dolce vita," a mix of romantic and eclectic melodies from all over the world. From Brazil to European cafés, the trio’s sound draws from various influences including pop, jazz, folk, classical, rock, and opera – all of it re-imagined so you will hear the melodies you love, or didn’t know you loved, like new again. title: Ismail Lumanovski & Alex Conde: Piano and Clarinet Improvisations
description: From Spain to San Francisco and Macedonia to New York City, Alex Conde and Ismail Lumanovski join together for an impassioned evening of original compositions. This duo of improvisers will explore the intersection of piano and clarinet, navigating their sonic homelands of jazz, balkan, classical and flamenco influences.rnrnDescribed by The New York Times as “a brilliant, fearless young clarinetist,” Macedonia-born Ismail Lumanovski brings classical training to his Balkan roots. Lumanovski studied at Julliard and has performed as a headlining artist around the globe. He founded and leads the NY Gypsy All Stars and co-founded both The Secret Trio and the Grneta Ensemble. Thriving on musical conversation, he has collaborated with bands like Inspector Gadje Balkan Brass Band, Ljubojna, as well as an international set of musicians including Trilok Gurtu, Erkan Ogur, Al Di Meola, and Husnu Senledirici.rnrnAfter graduating at the conservatory of music in Valencia, Spain, Alex Conde came to study at Berklee College in Boston with Danilo Perez, Joe Lovano and George Garzone. He has toured around the globe with flamenco companies and has appeared in various jazz festivals. His compositions for flamenco guitar and orchestra have been premiered in Miami with Jose Luis Rodriquez, Federico Britos and Omid Zoufonoun. Conde’s 2015 album, Descarga for Monk, a bold and creative tribute to the great Thelonious Monk, was released through ZOHO Music to rave reviews from prominent jazz critics such as Bill Milkowski.rnrnrnrnAdmission: $18 online / $15-25 door. Doors at 8:00pm. Show at 8:30pm. All ages welcome.rnrnrnrnJoin the conversation at the Poppy – volunteer with us! Click here.

title: David Tanenbaum Presents: Jon Mendle: 19th Century Virtuoso Guitar
description: Curated by classical guitarist David Tanenbaum, Jon Mendle is set to perform a repertoire of both forgotten gems and well-known concert works from the 19th century on original and reproduction 19th century guitars. The program will include works by Antoine de L'Hoyer, Napoléon Coste, Felix Horetzky, Giulio Regondi, and Johann Kaspar Mertz and will be performed in period-appropriate dress.rnrnJon Mendle made his Carnegie Hall debut at the age of 19. He received his Bachelor’s and Master’s degrees from the San Francisco Conservatory of Music where his teachers included David Tanenbaum, Lawrence Ferrara, Marc Teicholz, Dusan Bogdanovic, and Sergio Assad. Jon has performed throughout North America and Germany and has toured with Yo-Yo Ma and the Silk Road Ensemble. Released in January 2011 on In A Circle Records, his first album, L’infidele, was produced by his teacher and Latin Grammy-winner Sergio Assad.rnrnPreview Jon's music on YouTube.rnrnrnrnAdmission: $15-20. Doors at 3:30pm. Show at 4:00pm. All ages welcome.rnrnTickets will not be sold in advance for this event. Please arrive at the Red Poppy when doors open to purchase tickets.rnrnJoin the conversation at the Poppy – volunteer with us! Click here.
